data.title: Biggest stock movers Thursday: MPT, CVNA, CAR, and more
data.description:
Stock futures edged lower Thursday after the Federal Reserve's latest minutes revealed more hawkish tones than anticipated.

Here are some of Thursday's biggest stock movers:

Biggest stock gainers

  • Medical Properties Trust (MPT) +18% - Shares surged after Q4 results topped expectations and concerns tied to former key tenant Prospect Medical Holdings continued to fade. The REIT said it has largely resolved its exposure to Prospect’s restructuring, secured a new 15-year lease for its California hospitals expected to generate ~$45M in annual cash rent by late 2026, and is exiting remaining properties tied to the situation. Q4 revenue came in at $270.3M, above estimates and up sequentially and Y/Y, while cash collections from new tenants improved to $22M, or about 96% of scheduled rent.
  • Etsy (ETSY) +9% - Shares climbed after a modest improvement in GMS and ad revenue helped offset Q4 weaker profits and softer total revenue, while sentiment was also boosted by the announced $1.2B cash sale of its Depop marketplace to eBay. For the current quarter, Etsy expects GMS of $2.38B–$2.43B, up from $2.3B a year ago, with adjusted EBITDA margin projected at 28%–30%, improving from 26.3% last year.
  • Occidental Petroleum (OXY) +6% - Shares rose after beating Q4 profit estimates, boosting investor confidence with stronger finances and shareholder returns. The company raised its quarterly dividend by over 8% to $0.26 per share and cut debt by $5.8B following the January 2 completion of its OxyChem sale, bringing total principal debt down to about $15.0B and reinforcing its balance-sheet improvement story.
  • Figma (FIG) +3% - Shares jumped after strong Q4 results and upbeat guidance. Revenue rose 40% Y/Y to $303.8M, topping estimates, while net dollar retention hit 136%, weekly active users surged 70% Q/Q, and customers with over $1M in ARR climbed to 67. The company guided Q1 revenue to $315M–$317M, above the consensus of $292.5M, and FY2026 revenue to $1.36B–$1.37B, above the consensus of $1.29B, implying roughly 38% annual growth, alongside expected non-GAAP operating income of $100M–$110M.
  • DoorDash (DASH) +3% - Shares surged as strong order growth and upbeat guidance outweighed a Q4 top- and bottom-line miss. Orders climbed 32% to 903M, and GOV jumped 39% to $29.7B, both above expectations, while the Q1 outlook for GOV ($31B–$31.8B) and EBITDA ($675M–$775M) came in strong. Investors looked past softer margins and heavy investment spending, focusing on demand momentum and growth visibility.
  • eBay (EBAY) +4% - Shares advanced after delivering a Q4 beat on both revenue and earnings, supported by strong traction from its eBay Live holiday shopping events and ongoing AI-driven initiatives. The company also issued upbeat Q1 guidance, projecting revenue of $3.0B–$3.05B and non-GAAP EPS of $1.53–$1.59, both above consensus estimates. Additionally, eBay approved a new $2B share repurchase program and increased its quarterly cash dividend to $0.31 per share from $0.29.

Biggest stock losers

  • Klarna (KLAR) -26% - Shares tumbled despite strong Q4 results as the company warned GMV growth will slow in Q2 due to tough comps and a gradual rollout of new partnerships. The BNPL firm guided Q1 revenue to $900M–$980M (midpoint below estimates) and adjusted operating profit of $5M–$35M, well short of consensus. GMV is seen at $32B–$33B, and FY GMV is just above $155B, also trailing forecasts. While Klarna highlighted growth drivers like banking products and expanded partnerships with Worldpay, JPMorgan Payments, Stripe, and Nexi, it cautioned merchant activation will take time, weighing on near-term momentum.
  • Avis Budget Group (CAR) -21% - Shares dipped after Q4 results missed expectations, as ongoing repositioning efforts weighed on performance, including another charge tied to reducing the carrying value of its U.S. EV fleet. Management said it is tightening fleet discipline, strengthening the balance sheet, and improving customer experience as it heads into 2026 with a focus on more sustainable earnings growth.
  • Carvana (CVNA) -6% - Shares plunged as investors focused on profitability concerns despite strong Q4 volume and revenue growth. Sales jumped 58% Y/Y to a record $5.6B, and retail units topped expectations, but gross profit per vehicle declined, and wholesale profitability weakened. Adjusted EBITDA rose to $511M but missed estimates, margins narrowed to 9.1%, and the absence of near-term sales guidance added to caution, overshadowing the company’s long-term targets.

data.title: AppLovin moves higher as social media platform job posting gains attention
data.description:
• AppLovin (APP) shares moved higher by 2.5% on Thursday as a job posting discussing building a social media platform has gained attention.
  • The job posting, listed here, advertises for a software engineer to help build the company's “next-generation social platform.”
  • “You'll design and build robust, scalable systems that power everything from seamless media delivery and real-time social interactions to our intelligent content discovery engine,” the posting reads. “Your day might involve optimizing database queries to handle millions of concurrent content requests, designing new APIs for real-time engagement features, and implementing sophisticated services for content ecosystem management.”
  • Bloomberg initially reported on the job posting, notable given that AppLovin failed in trying to acquire the U.S. assets of TikTok.
  • AppLovin did not immediately respond to a request for comment from Seeking Alpha.

data.title: Appian outlines $50M stock buyback and projects 16% cloud revenue growth for 2026 amid AI-driven momentum
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Appian Corporation (APPN) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Matthew Calkins highlighted 2025 as a year of strong execution, noting "Appian's cloud subscriptions revenue grew 18% to $117.0 million. Subscriptions revenue grew 19% to $162.3 million. Total revenue grew 22% to $202.9 million. Adjusted EBITDA was $19.7 million." He emphasized a 50% increase in customers purchasing over $1 million of software, with nearly double the value of 7-figure transactions. Calkins described notable wins, including a European pharmaceutical research organization and a North American aerospace manufacturer, both signing 7-figure deals to leverage Appian's AI capabilities and process modernization.
  • Calkins reported strengthened U.S. public sector positioning, citing a new $500 million, 10-year enterprise agreement with the U.S. Army: "The Army is already an 8-figure ARR customer. This new framework allows it to purchase $500 million in Appian software and services over the next 10 years."
  • He announced a $50 million stock buyback and cited operational improvements: "We created $63 million in operating cash flow compared to a loss of $110 million 2 years ago."
  • Calkins stated, "AI use on our platform grew 14x year-over-year, and we are monetizing that growth. Customers must upgrade to Appian's AI license tier, which comes with an average price increase of 25%." He provided examples of customers upgrading for AI-driven automation and efficiency gains, such as a pharmaceutical company using Appian Doc Center to accelerate response times by 88%.
  • The company welcomed Dave Link to its Board of Directors, citing his expertise in scaling enterprise software companies and AI.
  • CFO Srdjan Tanjga introduced reporting changes and a new metric: "We are introducing a new metric, cloud net ARR expansion... Going forward, we will no longer report cloud gross renewal rate and net revenue retention."
  • Tanjga said, "Appian met or exceeded the guidance ranges we provided on our key metrics of cloud revenue, total revenue and adjusted EBITDA."

Outlook

  • Tanjga guided for Q1 2026 cloud subscription revenue between $119 million and $121 million, and total revenue between $189 million and $193 million. "Adjusted EBITDA for the first quarter of 2026 is expected to be between $19 million and $22 million. Non-GAAP earnings per share is expected to be between $0.16 and $0.20."
  • For full year 2026, "cloud subscription revenue is expected to be between $502 million and $510 million, representing year-over-year growth of 16% at the midpoint of the range. Total revenue is expected to be between $801 million and $817 million, representing year-over-year growth of 11% at the midpoint. Adjusted EBITDA is expected to range between $89 million and $99 million for an approximately 12% margin at the midpoint."
  • Guidance assumes professional services growth in the teens for Q1 and high single digits for the year, with FX expected to be a tailwind in Q1 and roughly neutral thereafter.

Financial Results

  • Cloud subscription revenue for Q4 was $117 million, an increase of 18% year-over-year; total subscription revenue reached $162.3 million, up 19%. Total revenue was $202.9 million, up 22% year-over-year.
  • Cloud net ARR expansion was 114% in Q4, up from 112% in Q3. The company reported 140 customers with $1 million plus of ARR, compared to 115 a year ago.
  • Non-GAAP gross margin was 73%; subscription gross profit margin was 86%. Professional services non-GAAP gross margin was 23%.
  • Adjusted EBITDA was $19.7 million; non-GAAP net income was $11.1 million or $0.15 per diluted share.
  • Cash and investments totaled $187.2 million at year-end. Cash provided by operations for the quarter was $1.1 million, and $62.9 million for the full year.

Q&A

  • Oscar Saavedra, Morgan Stanley, asked about the visibility behind Q1 guidance acceleration. Tanjga replied, "Q1...will benefit from strong new business that we had in Q4, which is why on a sequential basis is a robust guide."
  • Raimo Lenschow, Barclays, questioned Appian's strategic position as an AI process layer. Calkins responded, "We've been that layer for a long time...enables and governs digital workers."
  • Lenschow also asked about sales capacity. Tanjga said, "We've done a great job significantly improving our sales productivity...this return to growth of the sales org is the beginning of a long-term trend."
  • Steven Enders, Citi, inquired about AI's impact on customer budgets and pipeline. Calkins described AI as "an unalloyed positive for us in our relations with our customers...It's helped our TAM. It's helped our access. It's helped our win rate."
  • Enders asked about the Army agreement. Calkins said, "This is an important moment...It allows us to speak to any part of the organization with great credibility...a wonderful badge of seriousness."
  • James Wood, TD Cowen, asked about Appian's internal AI use and LLM vendor challenges. Calkins stated, "We're using it thoroughly...expecting major increases in all of our development capabilities this year because we're making a prolific use of AI."
  • Wood questioned professional services and on-prem strength. Tanjga explained, "Customers want to get the value but they're sensitive to get it at the levels of accuracy and performance...our success there and the change in how the government likes to deal with vendors has helped our professional services business."
  • Devin Au, KeyBanc, sought clarity on 2026 guidance conservatism. Tanjga replied, "There's no incremental conservatism or caution...cloud, it's just ratable. It's frankly a little bit easier to forecast."
  • Lucky Schreiner, D.A. Davidson, asked about deceleration in cloud guidance. Tanjga attributed it mainly to FX normalization.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts' tone was positive, emphasizing strong results and future growth, with repeated congratulations on the quarter. They raised questions about guidance visibility, AI opportunity, and sales investment, reflecting interest rather than skepticism.
  • Management's sentiment was confident and assertive, particularly during prepared remarks. Calkins described the Army agreement as a "wonderful badge of seriousness," and Tanjga highlighted consistent execution and strategic investments. In Q&A, management maintained a steady and detailed tone, addressing questions directly.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management conveyed greater confidence, with less discussion of macro uncertainty or conservatism.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Current quarter guidance language was more optimistic, with management highlighting sustained growth and the initiation of a capital return policy. The previous quarter referenced macro uncertainty and government shutdown disruptions.
  • Strategic focus shifted to scaling AI-driven revenue and expanding high-value enterprise deals, with the Army agreement marking a new milestone.
  • Analysts in both quarters focused on AI, public sector deals, and sales productivity, but the current quarter included more forward-looking questions about resource allocation and sales capacity growth.
  • Key metrics improved: cloud net ARR expansion rose to 114% from 112%, and $1 million ARR customers increased from 115 to 140. Management's tone indicated increased confidence in both profitability and pipeline.
  • The company introduced new financial reporting metrics and announced a $50 million stock buyback, signaling greater financial flexibility.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged investor concerns about AI as an existential threat to software firms, but Calkins emphasized Appian's positioning as essential to AI workflows.
  • FX impacts were noted as a factor in growth rates, with normalization expected after Q1 2026.
  • Tanjga highlighted a return to moderate investment pace in OpEx and the need for responsible scaling of sales and engineering capacity.
  • No new material business risks were raised by analysts.

Final Takeaway

Appian's Q4 2025 call demonstrated robust growth in cloud and total revenue, highlighted by a 50% rise in large enterprise deals and a major $500 million Army contract. The company guided for 16% cloud revenue growth for 2026, introduced new financial metrics, and announced a $50 million share buyback. Management emphasized the synergy between AI and process orchestration, with customer upgrades to AI tiers driving revenue and pipeline expansion. Analysts responded positively to the company's momentum, and management projected continued confidence and operational discipline as Appian enters a new phase of capital return and strategic investment.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: The U.S. trade deficit in 2025 stands at $901B
data.description:
The U.S. trade deficit for 2025 came in at $901.5 billion, down only 0.2%, or by $2.1 billion, in a year that saw the Trump administration announce a slew of tariffs. 

The U.S. international trade in goods and services deficit increased 32.6% to $70.3B in December vs. $55.50B consensus and $53.00B in November (revised from $56.8B), according to data released by the U.S. Census Bureau on Thursday.

The data comes after President Donald Trump announced a slew of aggressive tariffs, which looked to bring the deficit down. The president had looked to implement a series of reciprocal tariffs on certain countries that had surpluses against the U.S. 

Over the year, however, Trump also did roll back some tariffs, and talks were still ongoing with many countries to reach more trade deals. 

Exports were $3.43 trillion, up $199.8 billion from 2024, and imports were $4.3 trillion, up $197.8 billion from the year prior. 

More on the U.S. Economy

  • Some Fed members appear open to rate hike if inflation remains above target: FOMC minutes
  • Durable goods orders fall less than expected in December
  • Economic Data Alphabet Soup On The Menu: FOMC Minutes, GDP, PCE, PMIs, UMich

data.title: ‘We’re getting very strong disinflationary growth’ – counselor to Treasury Secretary Bessent
data.description:
Joe Lavorgna, Counselor to Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent, declared that the U.S. economy is experiencing “very strong disinflationary growth,” with GDP expanding at approximately 4% under President Trump’s administration.

“The reality… is we’re getting very strong disinflationary growth,” Lavorgna said in an interview with CNBC.

He emphasized that growth has been driven entirely by the private sector, noting that excluding government contributions, the economy is growing closer to 5%.

The administration’s fiscal discipline has also yielded significant results, according to Lavorgna. He pointed to a substantial drop in the calendar year budget deficit from nearly 7% to 5.4% of GDP, calling it the largest non-pandemic decline in the deficit-to-GDP ratio since 2013 when sequestration measures took effect.

To support this analysis, he mentioned headline and core CPI running at approximately 2.2% over the last four months. He attributed the cooling prices to a capital expenditure boom that is increasing productive capacity.

“All the ingredients now with faster productivity are there,” he said, adding that these conditions support lower interest rates going forward.

Addressing concerns about tariff-induced inflation, Lavorgna argued that the predicted price increases have not materialized in the data.

He cited research showing that the 90% pass-through rate predicted by the Federal Reserve has not occurred, with consumer goods prices running under 2% rather than the expected 4%.

“There’s clearly not that tariff effect that everybody thought was going to occur,” he said, suggesting that foreign producers are absorbing costs in their margins while tariff revenues have helped push mortgage rates lower.

Lavorgna acknowledged weakness in consumer confidence indicators but attributed them to a labor market that was “weaker than it appeared” when the administration took office.

He noted that non-farm employment has been revised down by nearly 2M jobs from 2023 to 2024. “We’re seeing kind of the mess we inherited… but the road of strong recovery is there,” he said, expressing confidence that job gains will accelerate.

Looking ahead, Lavorgna predicted rising after-tax incomes for Americans, particularly working-class families, due to tax reductions including the “no tax on tips” policy.

He expects that continued productivity gains combined with lower energy prices will increase overall affordability for U.S. households.

More on the U.S. economy

  • Wholesale inventories rise 0.2% M/M in December; retail inventories flat
  • Leading indicator index edges down 0.2% in December, as expected
  • Pending home sales unexpectedly fall in January
  • Minneapolis Fed's Kashkari sees monetary policy as 'pretty close to neutral'
  • Bullish sentiment slips as neutral outlook climbs in mid-February, shows latest AAII survey

data.title: Wedbush discounts threat of AI displacing enterprise software, particularly in security
data.description:
Wedbush Securities believes investor concerns over artificial intelligence models, such as those made by OpenAI (OPENAI) and Anthropic (ANTHRO), displacing enterprise software and cybersecurity providers stem from a "fear of the unknown."

"For the bulls, it's like fighting a ghost in a dark closet, as the numbers today cannot prove monetization yet for tech players while the cap-ex dollars approach $700 billion this year alone…and the fear from AI startups is this will be the moment of reckoning for the software industry," said Wedbush analysts, led by Dan Ives, in an investor note. 

"The fictional concept being debated in this market is that enterprise software and cyber security will not be needed by enterprises in the future…IT budgets can cut 70% of their budgets by going with cheaper AI tools," he added. "While this sounds like a good thesis on paper or on a whiteboard…the reality is dramatically different, as the complexity of enterprises today makes this a nearly impossible task."

He points to technological innovations over the past 25 years that were supposed to unravel sectors. For example, when Microsoft (MSFT) began offering cybersecurity products about 20 years ago, it was expected to wipe out most of the competition. Instead, a wide array of security companies have emerged and are a critical piece of enterprise budgets.

Despite numerous mergers and acquisitions in the sector, there are nearly 9,000 companies in the cybersecurity sector in the U.S. alone. The U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics projects a 33% increase in information security analyst jobs from 2023 to 2033.

"Palo Alto (PANW) is positioned squarely at the top of the cybersecurity mountain with this CyberArk deal to monetize the AI cyber TAM as it hits over the next 12 to 18 months, yet this morning investors are nitpicking the long-term AI vision vs. showing success right now," Ives noted. "In a nutshell, the future AI tech winners are being built while many investors will miss this narrative like other ones over the decades in tech."

data.title: Leading indicator index edges down 0.2% in December, as expected
data.description:
U.S. Leading Indicator: -0.2% M/M to 97.6 in December vs. -0.2% consensus and -0.3% in November and downwardly revised -0.2% decline in October, according to data released by The Conference Board on Thursday.

Coincident Index: +0.2% to 115.0 vs. +0.1% in November

Lagging Index: -0.1% to 119.6 vs. +0.1% in November.

Developing… Check back for updates.
